content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 7,
1994
Richard H.
Koppes
General Counsel
Public Employees’ Retirement System
Lincoln Plaza,
400
P Street
Sacramento,
CA
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—94-182
Dear Mr.
Koppes:
This is in reply to your request for advice.
You have asked
whether candidates for positions on the Public Employees’
Retirement System
(“PERS”)
Board subject to the restrictions of
Government Code Section 84308 if they are elected.
1
Since your question relates to specific PERS Board positions,
the advice herein will provide immunity under Section 83114(b) to
any past or future candidate for one of those positions.
OUESTION
Are contributions made to a candidate for election to the
PERS Board within the scope of Section 84308?
CONCLUSION
No.
Such contributions are not addressed by Section 84308.
FACTS
By statute, the PERS Board of Administration consists of
thirteen members.
Of these,
six are elected from the membership
under the Board’s supervision.
(Section 20100(g).)
Of the
remaining seven Board members,
two, the State Treasurer and the
State Controller,
hold office by virtue of holding statewide
elected office.
(Section 20100(c)
and (d).)
The rest of the
Board members hold office by virtue of appointment.
(Section
20100(a),
(b),
(e),
and (f).)
This request for advice pertains
1
Government Code Section 84308 is located in the Political
Reform Act
(Government Code Sections 81000-91015).
All statutory
references are to the Government Câ’de unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000—18954.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—94—182
Page
2
only to PERS Board members who hold PERS Board seats under Section
20100(g)
(election by the PERS membership).
The PERS Board is responsible for developing its own
procedures for the election of the six Board members elected from
the membership.
(Califoia Constitution Art.
XVI,
Section 17;
Section 20102.)
Accordingly, the PERS Board has enacted
regulations which govern its elections.
(See
2 Cal.
Code Regs.
§S554 et seq.)
It currently is amending those regulations.
The six PERS Board members elected from the membership are
elected by the following constituencies:
two are elected by
members of the system (i.e., elected by all active and retired
PERS members,
commonly referred to as the “members—at—large”);
one
is elected by active state members;
one is elected by active
school members;
one is elected by active local
(except school)
members;
and one is elected by retired members.
(Section
20100(g).)
Board members are elected for four year terms.
(Section 20101.)
If a seat becomes vacant during the term, the
PERS Board conducts a special election.
(Section 20100.)
PERS Bpard member terms are staggered.
In the first year of
the cycle,
an election is held for the three seats representing
active state,
school,
and local members.
In the second year,
an
election is held for the retiree representative.
There is no
election in the third year,
and,
in the fourth year,
an election
is held for the two member-at-large positions.
In the last member—at—large election, there were
approximately 876,000 potential voters.
In the last state,
school,
and local member elections, there were approximately
207,000,
218,000,
and 202,000 potential voters, respectively.
PERS projects that in the next retired member election, there will
be approximately 250,000 voters.
Under current regulations,
PERS
mails ballots directly to retired members,
and forwards ballots
for all active members to agency election officers for
distribution.
(See
2 Cal.
Code Regs.
§554.5.)
Under the new
proposed regulations,
PERS will mail ballots directly to all
potential voters.
PERS canvasses the returned ballots and the
Secretary of State certifies the results.
(See
2 Cal.
Code Regs.
§554. 6.)
The number of candidates for the PERS Board member seats
varies.
Typically, there are approximately 35 candidates in a
member—at—large election,
and less for the other elections.
There
were four candidates
(including incumbents)
in the last local
member election, thirteen candidates in the last state member
election, five in the last school member election,
and four
candidates in the last retired member election.
It takes approximately one year to conduct an election.
Typically, the voting portion of the election occurs between
October and November,
for seats which will be assumed the
following January.


File No.
A—94—182
Page
3
PERS is currently in the process of preparing for and
conducting an election for the three positions which represent
active state, school,
and local members.
During this process,
questions have arisen as to the restrictions which may apply to
candidates for PERS Board.-seats who accept contributions for
campaign purposes.
ANALYSIS
In the Koppes Advice Letter,
No. A-94-121,
we concluded that
the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”)
which
regulate campaign activities do not apply to PERS Board elections.
We also concluded that, generally, contributions to candidates for
the PERS Board were contributions under Section 82015, not gifts
subject to the Act’s reporting provisions and gift limits.
Based
upon this latter conclusion, you now ask if those same
contributions subject the PERS Board candidates to the
restrictions set forth in Section 84308 if they are elected to the
PERS Board.
Generally, Section 84308 restricts the voting of members of
boards and commissions if they have received contributions of more
than $250 from a party or participant in various proceedings
before that board or commission.
Members of the PERS Board are
subject to Section 84308.
However, the question you pose is
whether contributions to PERS Board members for election to the
PERS Board itself are “contributions” within the coverage of
Section 84308.
Subdivision
(a) (6)
of Section 84308 defines “contribution,”
for purposes of that section, as follows:
“Contribution” includes contributions to candidates and
committees in federal, state,
or local elections.
Furthermore, Section 82022 defines “election” as “any primary,
general, special or recall election held in this state.”
Reading these sections together, it appears that Section
84308’s restrictions were meant to apply only to those
contributions made in primary, general, special or recall
elections.
PERS Board elections do not fall within any of these
categories.
Furthermore,
as stated above,
in the Koppes Advice
Letter we concluded that PERS Board elections were not the type of
elections regulated by the Act’s campaign provisions.
On this
basis,
we conclude that contributions made to a candidate for
election to the PERS Board do not subject that candidate,
once
elected, to the restrictions of Section 84308.
This assumes that these contributions are made solely for the
purpose of supporting that candidate in that particular PERS Board
election.
Contributions made to a PERS Board candidate which
otherwise are for the purpose of supporting his or her candidacy


File No.
A—94—182
Page
4
in a primary, general or special election for another office or
which are to assist the candidate in a recall election relating to
another office,
do subject the candidate to Section 84308’s
restrictions.
The same applies to contributions directed or
solicited by that candidate for contribution to a candidate or
coimuittee in a primary, neral, special or recall election for
another office.
Finally,
we note that the remaining questions in your letter
are premised upon such contributions making Section 84308
applicable.
Since we do not find the section to be applicable,
we
do not need to address the additional questions.
Should you have any questions, please contact me at 916/322-
5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General
ounsel
By-f cott Halla nfl
Assistant General Counsel
SGC: SH:ak

sections.question:
Are contributions made to a candidate for election to the
PERS Board within the scope of Section 84308?
sections.conclusion:
No.
Such contributions are not addressed by Section 84308.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are contributions made to a candidate for election to the
PERS Board within the scope of Section 84308?

CONCLUSION: No.
Such contributions are not addressed by Section 84308.

OUESTION Are contributions made to a candidate for election to the PERS Board within the scope of Section 84308? CONCLUSION No. Such contributions are not addressed by Section 84308. FACTS By statute, the PERS Board of Administration consists of thirteen members. Of these, six are elected from the membership under the Board’s supervision. (Section 20100(g).) Of the remaining seven Board members, two, the State Treasurer and the State Controller, hold office by virtue of holding statewide elected office. (Section 20100(c) and (d).) The rest of the Board members hold office by virtue of appointment. (Section 20100(a), (b), (e), and (f).) The PERS Board is responsible for developing its own procedures for the election of the six Board members elected from the membership. (Califoia Constitution Art. XVI, Section 17; Section 20102.) Accordingly, the PERS Board has enacted regulations which govern its elections. (See 2 Cal. Code Regs. §S554 et seq.) It currently is amending those regulations. The six PERS Board members elected from the membership are elected by the following constituencies: two are elected by members of the system (i.e., elected by all active and retired PERS members, commonly referred to as the “members—at—large”); one is elected by active state members; one is elected by active school members; one is elected by active local (except school) members; and one is elected by retired members. PERS Bpard member terms are staggered. In the first year of the cycle, an election is held for the three seats representing active state, school, and local members.
